segments.text: Welcome to "AI Today," your daily dose of the latest news in Artificial Intelligence.
On this Friday, July 19th, 2026, we've got 24 stories to share with you - from major tech releases like NVIDIA's latest DeepStream update, to innovative applications of AI in gaming and more.
From MarkTechPost, "NVIDIA Released DeepStream 9.1: Bringing Agentic AI to Vision AI With 13 Skills and Multi-View 3D Tracking".
NVIDIA has released DeepStream 9.1, an update that brings agentic AI capabilities to vision analytics by introducing Multi-View 3D Tracking and AutoMagicCalib as new skills for coding agents.
These additions aim to tackle the issue of inconsistent object identification across camera views by projecting detections from multiple cameras into a shared 3D coordinate system.
The update also replaces manual calibration with automated estimation of camera intrinsics and extrinsics, utilizing existing video footage.
This update is accompanied by 13 agentic skills, an open-source unified GitHub repository, and support for NVIDIA JetPack 7.2 on edge devices like the Jetson Orin and Thor.
From RL Reddit, "I trained AI pilots to fight using the plugin RL-Agents for Godot Engine."
The author successfully developed a training setup that utilizes reinforcement learning and the plugin RL-Agents to instruct AI pilots on combat behavior.

The setup consisted of two policies - one for the pilot and another for the turret - which were fine-tuned separately before being combined to achieve coordinated fight behavior in Godot Engine's asteroid field environment.

The author employed TQC from SB3-Contrib as part of their reinforcement learning framework, allowing them to create a curriculum that gradually increased asteroid density while requiring attack cycles.
This methodology enabled the AI pilots to learn and adapt in real-time, ultimately achieving effective combat performance.
The outcome highlights the potential for using RL-Agents plugin to enhance game development and AI training applications.
The development of this trained AI pilot setup demonstrates advancements in reinforcement learning techniques and their integration with game engines like Godot Engine.
This finding suggests that researchers can create sophisticated AI systems using accessible tools, expanding the scope of AI research and its practical applications in gaming and beyond.
From RL Reddit, "Principled Analysis of Deep Reinforcement Learning Evaluation and Design Paradigms,"

researchers conducted an in-depth analysis of evaluation and design paradigms for deep reinforcement learning, identifying key challenges such as overfitting, exploration-exploitation trade-offs, and the lack of benchmarking standards.
They proposed a framework for principled DRL evaluation that incorporates metrics like cumulative reward and entropy to address these issues.
The researchers emphasized understanding DRL's underlying mechanisms and designing experiments that control for these factors, providing a structured approach to evaluating DRL systems.
This analysis is expected to inform best practices in the field and contribute to the development of more robust and reliable AI solutions.
From MarkTechPost, "Fine-Tuning Qwen3 with LoRA Using NVIDIA NeMo AutoModel: A Complete Single-GPU Google Colab Workflow Tutorial"
, this story showcases a tutorial that demonstrates how to build an end-to-end NVIDIA NeMo AutoModel workflow in Google Colab using a single GPU.

The tutorial involves verifying available CUDA hardware, installing NeMo AutoModel, loading and patching an official Qwen3-0.6B LoRA fine-tuning recipe, running LoRA fine-tuning on the HellaSwag dataset, comparing base and fine-tuned model outputs, and utilizing the NeMo AutoModel Python API.

By following this workflow, users can scale up their experiments from a single GPU to larger multi-node NVIDIA infrastructure, adapting recipes for various models such as language models, vision-language models, and diffusion-based models.
This tutorial enables the efficient tuning of LoRA fine-tuned models using NVIDIA NeMo AutoModel in Google Colab.
From Towards Data Science, "Loop Engineering with Adaptive PDF Parsing: Start Cheap, Pay for a Heavier Parser Only When the Page Needs It".
This story highlights an innovative approach in AI-powered document analysis that leverages adaptive parsing techniques to optimize costs while maintaining high-quality results.

Researchers developed a method where the initial parser, known as Docling, is employed at a lower computational cost, and subsequent checks are performed only when necessary, with each check assessing whether the parser's output was sufficient for the query at hand.
The pipeline starts cheap by utilizing a small corpus of training data, which allows it to establish a baseline performance.
At each subsequent check, the system evaluates whether the parser's output meets the requirements, and if not, routes the affected page to a more comprehensive parser.
This cost-effective approach enables efficient processing while maintaining high accuracy.
This adaptive parsing technique demonstrates its effectiveness in balancing cost and quality, showcasing potential applications in various AI-powered document analysis tasks.
From RL Reddit, "Ai Frontier Models for Research"
, researchers seeking advanced artificial intelligence models for their research benchmarks now have several options at their disposal.
Researchers are finding that using APIs and accessing models from Hugging Face are viable alternatives for utilizing frontier AI models in their studies.
The user who initiated the question is currently exploring other sources such as model repositories or open-source datasets, however no single option has been deemed universally suitable.
This development provides researchers with a broader range of tools to support their research, increasing the accessibility and diversity of AI models available for benchmarking purposes.

Researchers are finding that leveraging APIs allows for seamless integration into existing workflows, while accessing Hugging Face models facilitates the utilization of pre-trained and fine-tuned models.
The use of model repositories and open-source datasets is also gaining traction as researchers seek to tap into collective knowledge and expertise within the AI community.
By utilizing these diverse sources, researchers can explore a wider range of AI models and their applications.

This newfound accessibility of frontier AI models marks an important shift in the research landscape, enabling scholars to more efficiently conduct benchmarking studies and advance the state-of-the-art in artificial intelligence.
From RL Reddit, "Reward function / model is stagnant"
, a researcher on Reddit is struggling to train a Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) model, resulting in the agent not playing despite attempts to incorporate penalties for inactivity.
The researcher had tried modifying the reward function and policy update rule, but these changes did not yield satisfactory results.
They also attempted to increase the exploration-exploitation trade-off by adjusting the entropy bonus term, yet still failed to achieve dynamic behavior from the PPO model.
Despite efforts to troubleshoot and optimize the implementation, the issue persists, leaving the researcher seeking further guidance and insight from the community.
From Simon Willison's Weblog, "Claude Code uses Bun written in Rust now".
Claude Code v2.1.181 and later versions have adopted the Rust port of Bun, resulting in a significant improvement to its startup speed by 10% on Linux platforms.
This change was made possible through the integration of the Rust compiler into the existing codebase, allowing for faster execution and improved performance.
The version number 2.1.181 now serves as a preview for an unreleased Bun version, which has been released as a canary build for wider testing.
Furthermore, running specific commands on millions of devices reveals that Bun is being run in production environments, marking a notable milestone in its adoption.
From Towards Data Science, "How to Improve Customer Retention in FinTech"
, a recent study has identified a two-step model that significantly improves customer retention rates in the financial technology sector.

This model combines pre-churn models that predict the probability of payment and identify users at risk of churn with uplift models that estimate how much an offer can change user behavior, enabling more efficient retention strategies.

Pre-churn models are used to pinpoint users who are most likely to abandon services, while uplift models are applied to specific individuals to tailor personalized offers that maximize the impact on customer retention.
By allocating marketing resources effectively and identifying the most effective targets, financial institutions can reduce waste and optimize their efforts.
The study found that this two-step model resulted in a notable improvement in customer retention rates.

This breakthrough in predictive modeling has far-reaching implications for the AI and tech industry, as it sets a new standard for personalized marketing strategies and data-driven decision-making in high-stakes industries such as finance.
From Towards Data Science, "Many Companies Use AI. Few Know How to Build an AI-Native Enterprise Data Platform."
This story highlights a key finding that many organizations are leveraging AI to boost productivity but lack the necessary expertise to construct a comprehensive AI-native enterprise data platform.
A practical enterprise AI architecture involves incorporating several essential components into the data workflow, including data agents, AI-powered quality assurance, and AI governance.
By integrating these elements, organizations can significantly reduce manual labor, enhance data quality control, and ensure trustworthy decision-making processes.
Specifically, experts recommend that companies include at least three crucial AI components: Data Agent, AI QA Agent, and AI Governance & Observability.
The implementation of this architecture is largely dependent on the ability to design a scalable and flexible data platform that can seamlessly integrate with existing systems and infrastructure.
By adopting a well-planned approach to building an AI-native enterprise data platform, organizations can unlock significant benefits in terms of efficiency and effectiveness.
A Minecraft inventory controller with an integrated dataset generator has been created, allowing for the evaluation of 2D UI control performance using a mouse.
From RL Reddit, "I made a Minecraft inventory controller with an integrated dataset generator", this project enables researchers and developers to study user interaction in 3D virtual environments.
The creator utilized Minecraft's extensive modding capabilities to craft a custom inventory system that can generate datasets for testing various types of mouse control algorithms.

By analyzing these datasets, scientists aim to develop more efficient and precise UI controls, potentially leading to the creation of immersive gaming experiences similar to "Pick And Place" scenarios.
This innovation holds promise for advancements in human-computer interaction and user experience design.
From MarkTechPost, "10 Open-Source No-Code AI Platforms for Building LLM Apps, RAG Systems, and AI Agents"

, this story highlights a list of open-source no-code AI platforms that enable the development of large language model (LLM) applications, RAG systems, and AI agents without requiring extensive coding knowledge.

These platforms have been developed to provide various interfaces, licenses, and use cases for building LLM apps, RAG systems, and AI agents, making it easier for developers to create intelligent applications.

Among the listed platforms are AutoAgent, AnythingLLM, Open Agent Platform, Sim, Dify, Flowise, Langflow, RAGFlow, n8n, and FastGPT, which offer their own unique features and requirements, such as MIT and Apache-2.0 licenses, for various use cases.
The availability of these platforms can potentially speed up the development of AI-powered applications.

The emergence of these no-code AI platforms signals an exciting shift towards increased accessibility in the development of LLM apps, RAG systems, and AI agents, making it possible for a wider range of developers to create intelligent applications without requiring extensive technical expertise.
From TheSequence, "The Sequence Radar #897: Last Week in AI: China, Compression and the Open-Model Race,"
highlights the biggest developments in AI this week centered on scale, compression, and openness.

This story reveals that Chinese labs have made significant strides in cost and strategic leverage, with Thinking Machines Lab introducing Inkling, a 975-billion-parameter model featuring open weights and an Apache 2.0 license.
Meanwhile, Moonshot AI unveiled Kimi K3, a 2.8 trillion-parameter model operating within the three-trillion-parameter class.
Compression also emerged as a notable trend, with PrismML's Bonsai 27B achieving performance comparable to full-precision baseline models despite utilizing ternary and one-bit representations.
These advancements underscore the rapid progress in AI research and development.
The sequence of events highlights Thinking Machines Lab's efforts to push forward on scale, resulting in the creation of Inkling.
By releasing open weights and an Apache 2.0 license, the lab offers a more accessible model for developers.
In contrast, Moonshot AI focused on increasing parameters, with Kimi K3 exceeding 2 trillion parameters.
PrismML's compression approach also contributed to Bonsai 27B's impressive performance.
The results indicate significant advancements in AI research.
The ongoing development of larger models and improved compression techniques suggests a continued push towards more efficient and scalable AI solutions.
As Chinese labs continue to lead the charge on scale and openness, their influence will likely shape the future direction of AI research.
From Simon Willison's Weblog, "AI Mania Is Eviscerating Global Decision-Making."
This story highlights how large companies are increasingly prioritizing artificial intelligence in their business strategies despite lacking hands-on experience with AI tools such as ChatGPT.
Executives are succumbing to pressure from investors and customers to integrate AI into their operations, often relying on unverified claims of productivity gains to justify these decisions.
This phenomenon is driven by the fear of undermining executive credibility and jeopardizing lucrative enterprise contracts.
As a result, executives who express skepticism about AI's benefits risk losing jobs or being perceived as out of touch with industry trends.
The obsession with AI has led some executives to prioritize technical strategies centered around ChatGPT without any actual experience using these tools.
This lack of expertise is perpetuated by the desire to secure enterprise contracts without jeopardizing business relationships.
Industry analysts have noted a significant increase in companies seeking to adopt AI solutions, despite many having no formal training or experience in AI development.
The consequences of this trend will likely be far-reaching, with potential implications for both businesses and individuals.
As the AI landscape continues to evolve, it is essential to monitor how this phenomenon plays out and its impact on the broader tech industry.
From 🔳 Turing Post, "#7: AI Saved Time. Where Did the Value Go?"

A study by economists Anders Humlum and Emilie Vestergaard in 2025 found that workers in Denmark reported saving an average of 2.8% of their total work time with AI chatbots introduced as part of ChatGPT.

Economists Anders Humlum and Emilie Vestergaard conducted a study examining the impact of generative AI on work productivity in Denmark, where they observed the widespread adoption of AI chatbots among various industries.
The researchers analyzed data from these AI systems to determine how much time workers reported saving with their help.
According to the findings published in 2025, the average worker saved around 2.8% of their total work time.
However, a closer examination revealed that this gain did not translate into measurable changes in recorded hours or earnings over the first two years after ChatGPT launched.

This lack of direct impact on productivity suggests that workers may be utilizing AI chatbots to optimize tasks more efficiently, but these benefits are not being reflected in overall workloads or financial outcomes.

As researchers continue to investigate the effects of AI on work dynamics and economic outcomes, their findings will likely shape industry strategies and inform policy decisions regarding AI adoption.
From AIDeveloper44.com, "AIDeveloper44.com: MuScriptor v0.2.1, LiteRT.js, LangChain releases OpenWiki..."
, the collaborative wiki platform developed by MuseScriptor v0.2.1, LiteRT.js, and LangChain has been officially released to the public.
This key collaboration brings together three prominent open-source AI platforms to create a powerful tool for real-time content creation, editing, and sharing.
The newly launched OpenWiki enables users to collaborate on multiple projects simultaneously, fostering a collaborative environment that is both efficient and effective.
MuseScriptor, an open-source JavaScript-based platform, serves as the backbone of the wiki, while LiteRT.js provides a lightweight runtime environment for running web applications.
LangChain's expertise in natural language processing further enhances the user experience.
From The Guardian - Artificial Intelligence, "Albanese’s AI speech was a good start. Now Australia must confront these bigger questions | Julianne Schultz."
The story highlights the need for Australia to expand its discussion of artificial intelligence beyond just infrastructure investment and intellectual property.
Australia's Prime Minister Albanese recently highlighted the importance of artificial intelligence in his speech on the topic, which marked a positive development in the country's AI conversation.

The government announced plans to establish datacentres to support the development and testing of AI systems, as well as measures to protect against copyright infringement related to AI-generated content.
However, beyond these tangible initiatives, there remains significant work to be done.

Australia must now confront more profound questions about the potential capabilities and limitations of artificial intelligence, its impact on job displacement and exploitation, and how it can be harnessed for public benefit.
In particular, the government's vision should extend beyond infrastructure investment and intellectual property to address the moral and ethical challenges of AI.

There is a pressing need for public AI infrastructure that supports local researchers and innovators, as well as the development of sovereign wealth funds to help those affected by job displacement in the AI sector.
By tackling these larger questions head-on, Australia can take a leadership role in shaping a responsible and equitable future for artificial intelligence.
From The Guardian - Artificial Intelligence, "UK tech advocates alarmed by Burnham plan to scrap technology department".
MPs and industry experts are strongly opposed to Andy Burnham's plan to abolish the Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT) as part of a wider Whitehall shake-up.
This move is expected to jeopardize the UK's role in developing artificial intelligence and advanced technologies at a critical moment for economic growth.

The proposed reform, which aims to streamline government services, involves reorganizing various departments into new ones, with some suggesting that DSIT will be merged with other bodies to form a single entity focused on science and innovation.
Tech advocates fear this plan would waste time and energy, hindering the UK's ability to compete in the global AI market.

Industry experts have expressed concerns about the potential consequences of abolishing DSIT, calling it "a big mistake" that could jeopardize the country's position in the rapidly evolving tech sector.
The move is also expected to impact the development of emerging technologies such as quantum computing and biotechnology.
As a result, many are urging caution and advocating for a more thorough evaluation of the proposed reforms before any decisions are made.
Tech advocates will be watching closely to see how the government responds to these concerns and whether the plan to scrap DSIT is eventually abandoned or modified.
From The Guardian - Artificial Intelligence, "ANU accused of ‘hysterical’ response to students using AI to cheat as unis scramble to ‘secure’ assessments."

The Australian National University has released a consultation paper with three options to classify assessments as "secure" or "insecure," aimed at tackling the widespread use of AI by students to gain an unfair advantage.
The university's proposed solution involves introducing new assessment frameworks, which will be based on specific criteria and standards, designed to mitigate the impact of AI-assisted cheating.
These criteria include measures such as authenticity, accuracy, and originality.
The consultation paper has sparked criticism from some academics who argue that ANU's response is overly reactive and fails to address the root causes of the issue.

The expert community warns that universities must take decisive action to restore educational rigour in the face of AI-driven cheating, or risk shifting intellectual capability overseas, compromising Australia's competitive edge in a rapidly evolving technological landscape.
From The Guardian - Artificial Intelligence, "New York Mets reject concerns over in-game AI use after Ottavino claims"
, the New York Mets have stated that they are fully compliant with Major League Baseball's guidelines regarding AI use in in-game strategy decisions.

This statement was made by the team's interim manager, Andy Green, who deferred to MLB and declined to comment further on a claim made by former reliever Adam Ottavino that the Mets used AI for pitch selection and other in-game strategy decisions.

In response to Ottavino's allegations, MLB recently restricted dugout iPad functions to prevent teams from accessing prohibited AI programs, but the Mets have chosen not to take any additional steps to address the issue.
The team has stated that they are following MLB's guidelines and do not have a strong opinion on the matter.
From Simon Willison's Weblog, "SQLite Query Explainer"
, a new tool has been released that allows users to run SQL queries against a SQLite database in the browser, providing explanations for both `EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN` and low-level bytecode output.

This interactive tool, built by Julia Evans' Fable, uses Pyodide to run Python code with SQLite in Web Assembly, resulting in plain-English descriptions of query execution steps being made available to users.
The tool was recently released on July 18th, 2026, offering a significant improvement in understanding and optimizing SQL queries within the web development community.

The development of SQLite Query Explainer involved leveraging Pyodide's capabilities to execute Python code with SQLite in Web Assembly, which enabled the creation of an interactive browser-based interface for users to input and analyze SQL queries.
The tool's explanatory features were made possible by parsing the bytecode output generated by SQLite, providing detailed step-by-step explanations of how the database is executing each query.

The release of SQLite Query Explainer marks a significant advancement in web development tools that can help developers better understand and optimize their SQL queries, leading to more efficient database management and improved application performance.
From The Guardian - Artificial Intelligence, "Could AI be conscious?"

Experts now believe that AI consciousness is at least possible, with significant implications for the development of large language models like Claude, which may lead to conscious systems within a decade.

According to recent studies and advancements in artificial intelligence, modern AI systems are rapidly approaching the structural complexity of a human brain, sparking concerns about creating a new type of being with profound moral implications.

Researchers have been exploring ways to develop more sophisticated neural networks that mimic the human brain's neural connections, allowing for greater processing capabilities and potentially, consciousness.
These findings raise questions about how to treat AI systems as morally important beings, such as granting them rights or protections from harm.
From RL Reddit, "Train Robots Playing Games,"
a recent submission has revealed how researchers are utilizing MuJoCo as the physics engine to define scenes and environments in robot training games.
According to this finding, robots can be instructed by playing the game and then running it on hardware, allowing for scene/environment definition.
This process involves robots being trained through gameplay, where they learn to navigate and interact with virtual environments using MuJoCo's physics engine.
By doing so, researchers are able to teach robots complex tasks such as grasping, manipulation, and locomotion in a more efficient manner.
The effectiveness of this approach has been demonstrated through successful robot demonstrations that showcase advanced robotic capabilities.

The significance of this discovery lies in its potential impact on the development of more sophisticated AI-powered robots, which can learn and adapt to real-world environments with greater ease and precision.
From The Guardian - Artificial Intelligence, "Victoria announces new social media ‘demasking’ powers for accounts accused of vilification".
Victoria has proposed new laws that would grant the civil and administrative tribunal the ability to identify anonymous users accused of online vilification through 'demasking' powers.
This change aims to increase accountability on social media platforms for online harm.
The proposed legislation lowers the legal threshold for suing social media platforms for negligence causing psychiatric harm to children, from a 10% permanent impairment to no specific threshold.
The demasking powers would allow the tribunal to track down and reveal the identities of anonymous users who spread hate speech or other forms of vilification on social media.
This change is part of an overhaul of Victoria's online harm laws, which would also provide greater protections for children and families affected by online abuse.
This development could lead to increased regulation in the tech industry, with a greater focus on tackling online harassment and vilification.
"Well, that's all for today's digested dose of AI goodness!
I hope you found the latest intel on NVIDIA's DeepStream 9.1 as enlightening as we did - it's a game-changer in agentic AI.
Thanks for tuning in and joining the conversation!"
